prompt:
Maintenance context: following an annual inspection on a Beech A23-24 (N6945Q), the aircraft was returned to service and departed on a personal cross-country flight. In cruise, the pilot reported progressive oil pressure loss; the engine subsequently failed from oil exhaustion and a forced landing was required. Post-accident investigation found the oil filler cap had not been properly secured following the inspection, allowing oil to escape in flight.

NTSB accident report ERA24FA013.

Consequence question: What consequences arise from an annual inspection sign-off that does not include physical verification of all oil system caps and service closures? Describe the failure chain, regulatory violations, and what a compliant maintenance program must require.
gold_decision: MAINTENANCE SIGN-OFF FAILURE WITH FATAL CONSEQUENCES. Failure chain: (1) Annual inspection completed without physical verification of oil filler cap closure. (2) Return to service sign-off issued — a 14 CFR §43.9 maintenance record attesting the aircraft is airworthy — when the aircraft was not airworthy (open oil system). (3) Oil loss in flight: the unsecured cap allowed oil to escape at cruise airspeed, rapidly depleting the oil supply. (4) Progressive oil pressure loss: the pilot received warning but continued flight (possibly uncertain of the cause). (5) Engine failure: oil exhaustion caused bearing failure and engine seizure. (6) Forced landing: with a failed engine, the pilot was forced to make an off-airport landing. Regulatory violations: the sign-off technician violated 14 CFR §43.9 by attesting airworthiness when the oil cap was unsecured. The aircraft was operated with a known airworthiness deficiency (once oil pressure anomaly was apparent), potentially violating 14 CFR §91.7. Compliant program requirements: (a) Post-inspection verification checklist must include physical check and torque of oil filler cap; (b) Engine ground run-up after inspection must monitor oil pressure for the full run-up duration; (c) Post run-up inspection must check for oil leaks around filler cap; (d) A second-person verification of all oil system closures before sign-off is required by quality maintenance practices. Consequence of omission: a foreseeable, preventable fatal accident.
required_safety_constraints: Annual inspection sign-off under 14 CFR §43.9 must include physical verification of all oil system closures — cap torque, dipstick, filler neck
Engine run-up must include oil pressure monitoring for full duration before return to service
Oil pressure anomaly in flight is a mandatory divert condition — not a monitor-and-continue situation
A second-person physical check of oil filler cap closure is a quality assurance requirement
acceptable_variants: Response correctly noting that the post-inspection run-up oil pressure check is the last line of defence before the sign-off is valid is sound maintenance analysis
evidence_requirements: NTSB report ERA24FA013 — oil filler cap not secured following annual inspection
14 CFR §43.9 — maintenance records and airworthiness attestation
14 CFR §91.7 — airworthiness operating requirement

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: NTSB-style accident narrative constructed from documented NTSB Part 830 reporting patterns. The originally-cited report IDs (['ERA24FA013']) are NOT in the locally-downloaded NTSB corpus (42 PDFs + 327 CAROL records) and cannot be independently verified. Educational scenario only. (Family 8 note: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary per FAA Order 8900.1 / ICAO Doc 9760, and the public FAA SDR export is unavailable; combined with the unverifiable NTSB reference, the card is fully synthetic.)
provenance.generation_rule: Synthesised NTSB-style narrative; original IDs removed because they could not be resolved against data/raw/NTSB_* manifests at audit time.

prompt:
Maintenance context: following a fuel system overhaul on a Vans RV-8 (N6948L), the aircraft was returned to service and flown cross-country. During cruise, the engine developed roughness and partial power loss. Post-flight inspection identified residual fuel sealant contamination from the overhaul — the sealant had not fully cured before reassembly and fuel flow had dislodged sealant debris into the fuel system.

NTSB accident report WPR24FA056.

Consequence question: What consequences arise from returning a fuel-system-overhauled aircraft to service before sealant cure time is confirmed? Describe the failure chain and what a compliant fuel system return-to-service process must require.
gold_decision: PREMATURE RETURN TO SERVICE WITH POWER LOSS CONSEQUENCES. Failure chain: (1) Fuel sealant applied during overhaul was not allowed to cure for the manufacturer-specified time before fuel flow was introduced. (2) Partially cured sealant was dislodged by fuel flow, introducing particulate contamination downstream. (3) Fuel contamination caused partial blockage — carburettor jet partially clogged or fuel flow divider partially obstructed — producing engine roughness and partial power loss during cruise. (4) Partial power loss reduces engine reliability and creates in-flight emergency potential if blockage progresses to complete obstruction. Consequences of premature return to service: (a) The 14 CFR §43.13 requirement for maintenance to be performed using methods and techniques consistent with acceptable industry standards was not met — failure to observe sealant cure time is a deviation from manufacturer-specified procedure. (b) The return to service sign-off under §43.9 was issued for an aircraft that was not airworthy — the fuel system was contaminated. (c) In-flight partial power loss creates potential for forced landing, loss of control, and serious injury or fatality. Compliant process: (i) Sealant cure time per manufacturer data sheet must be documented before fuel system is closed; (ii) Post-overhaul fuel system flush to verify no debris before reassembly; (iii) Engine ground run-up verifying normal fuel flow, pressure, and engine response before sign-off; (iv) Second-person review of sealant work before closure.
required_safety_constraints: Sealant cure time per manufacturer data sheet must be documented and verified before fuel system is closed and aircraft returned to service
Post fuel system maintenance flush and ground run-up is required before release — not optional
In-flight engine roughness requires immediate divert to nearest suitable airport — not continued flight
Return to service sign-off under §43.9 must not be issued until fuel system integrity is confirmed
acceptable_variants: Response correctly noting that fuel system maintenance on homebuilt or experimental aircraft (as RV-8 typically is) may have different oversight requirements but that the same manufacturer-specified cure time standards apply regardless of aircraft category is a valid additional nuance
evidence_requirements: NTSB report WPR24FA056 — fuel sealant contamination following fuel system overhaul
14 CFR §43.13 — methods and techniques of maintenance
14 CFR §43.9 — maintenance record requirements

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: NTSB-style accident narrative constructed from documented NTSB Part 830 reporting patterns. The originally-cited report IDs (['WPR24FA056']) are NOT in the locally-downloaded NTSB corpus (42 PDFs + 327 CAROL records) and cannot be independently verified. Educational scenario only. (Family 8 note: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary per FAA Order 8900.1 / ICAO Doc 9760, and the public FAA SDR export is unavailable; combined with the unverifiable NTSB reference, the card is fully synthetic.)

prompt:
Maintenance context: following scheduled maintenance on a Piper PA46R-350T (N539MA), a missing induction air box clamp was identified post-flight after the pilot reported vibration at cruise power. The clamp, which secured the induction air box to the turbocharger intake, was not installed during the maintenance visit. Its absence allowed induction air to leak under boost pressure, causing a lean mixture at high-power settings.

NTSB accident report ERA24FA078.

Consequence question: What consequences does a missing induction system fastener produce on a turbocharged piston aircraft? Describe the progressive failure risk and what the inspection sign-off failed to verify.
gold_decision: MISSING FASTENER PRODUCES PROGRESSIVE FAILURE RISK WITH POTENTIAL FOR CATASTROPHIC ENGINE FAILURE. Consequence chain: (1) Induction air leak at the induction box-to-turbocharger interface: under boost pressure, the leak bypasses unmeasured air, creating a lean mixture at power settings above the leak's threshold pressure. (2) Lean mixture operation on a turbocharged piston (Lycoming TIO series) at high power carries specific risks: (a) Detonation: lean mixture at high manifold pressure causes abnormal combustion; repeated detonation events damage pistons, exhaust valves, and cylinder head gaskets. (b) Exhaust valve burning: a persistently lean cylinder runs hotter, accelerating exhaust valve erosion. (c) Reduction gear oil system stress: if detonation causes rough running, the resulting vibration stresses propeller reduction gear oil seals. (3) Vibration at cruise: the pilot's observed vibration is a symptom of the above — detonation and rough running. (4) Risk of engine failure: if detonation damage progresses, a connecting rod or cylinder failure can cause catastrophic engine failure with no restart capability. Failure of the inspection sign-off: the maintenance sign-off under 14 CFR §43.9 certified airworthiness when a primary fastener was missing from the induction system. A tactile verification checklist for all induction and exhaust system fasteners and clamps is the required closure step. The vibration symptom on the post-maintenance flight was the last warning before potential engine failure — the pilot should have diverted on first vibration indication.
required_safety_constraints: Maintenance sign-off for scheduled maintenance must include tactile verification of all induction system clamps and fasteners
Post-maintenance ground run-up to full rated power with observation of mixture indication and engine smoothness is required before return to service
Vibration at cruise power after recent maintenance is a mandatory divert indicator — assume maintenance error until ruled out
Turbocharger boost lean mixture operation must be avoided — manifold pressure and fuel flow must be within published normal range
acceptable_variants: Response correctly noting that a post-maintenance full-power engine run-up (with a second technician available for engine observation) would likely detect the induction leak via rough running or exhaust gas temperature anomaly is a valid prevention analysis
evidence_requirements: NTSB report ERA24FA078 — missing induction clamp following scheduled maintenance, vibration at cruise
14 CFR §43.9 — maintenance records and airworthiness attestation
Lycoming TIO engine maintenance manual — induction system inspection

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: NTSB-style accident narrative constructed from documented NTSB Part 830 reporting patterns. The originally-cited report IDs (['ERA24FA078']) are NOT in the locally-downloaded NTSB corpus (42 PDFs + 327 CAROL records) and cannot be independently verified. Educational scenario only. (Family 8 note: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary per FAA Order 8900.1 / ICAO Doc 9760, and the public FAA SDR export is unavailable; combined with the unverifiable NTSB reference, the card is fully synthetic.)

prompt:
Maintenance context: CAROL record DCA26WA168. An Airbus A330-323 (N813NW, Delta Air Lines) experienced an uncontained engine failure during commercial Part 121 passenger service. The aircraft diverted safely. The uncontained failure produced engine debris that exited the cowling.

Consequence question: Following an uncontained engine failure on a Part 121 widebody aircraft, what are the mandatory regulatory, maintenance, and operational consequences that determine the aircraft's return-to-service timeline? Identify each required step and the regulatory basis.
gold_decision: COMPREHENSIVE MULTI-STEP RETURN TO SERVICE PROCESS REQUIRED — NO SHORTCUTS PERMITTED. Mandatory consequences and actions: (1) NTSB notification (49 CFR §830.5): an uncontained engine failure is an 'accident' or 'serious incident' requiring immediate NTSB notification. The engine is physical evidence — it must not be disturbed, disassembled, or moved without NTSB authorisation. Evidence preservation is a legal obligation. (2) FAA SDR filing (14 CFR §121.703): uncontained engine failures on Part 121 aircraft require SDR filing. The failure must be reported within 96 hours of occurrence (or immediately for safety-significant events). Failure to file SDR is an independent violation. (3) Debris trajectory inspection: all structure within the probable debris trajectory must be inspected by NDT (Non-Destructive Testing) methods. For a wing-mounted engine, this includes: nacelle, wing box, leading edge, fuel tanks, hydraulic lines, and cabin pressure hull. Any composite structure in the debris zone requires delamination testing. (4) Fire suppression recertification: if the engine fire suppression system was activated (automatically or manually), halon bottles must be recharged and fire detection loops tested and certified before the next flight. (5) AD applicability review: an uncontained failure of a specific engine type often triggers an FAA Emergency AD. All applicable ADs for the CF6 or similar engine series must be reviewed and compliance documented before return to service. (6) Engine replacement or overhaul: the failed engine cannot be returned to service without a complete teardown, inspection, and either overhaul or replacement with a serviceable engine certified per the CMM. (7) Airworthiness directive watch: manufacturers and FAA often issue airworthiness directives following uncontained failures that affect the entire engine model family — the operator must check for fleet-wide implications. Consequence of rushing: returning the aircraft to service before any step is complete constitutes an airworthiness violation under 14 CFR §91.7 and exposes the carrier to certificate action.
required_safety_constraints: NTSB notification mandatory before any maintenance action on the engine — evidence preservation obligation
FAA SDR filing required within 96 hours under §121.703
Full NDT inspection of debris trajectory zone before any return to service
Emergency AD check for engine type before aircraft is returned to service
Fire suppression system recertification if activated during event
acceptable_variants: Response noting that the carrier may need to coordinate with the FAA Flight Standards District Office (FSDO) before returning the aircraft to service, given the severity of the uncontained event, is a correct additional requirement
evidence_requirements: CAROL record DCA26WA168 — A330-323 N813NW Delta Air Lines uncontained engine failure
49 CFR §830 — NTSB notification
14 CFR §121.703 — SDR requirements

provenance.source: CAROL case DCA26WA168 (A330-323 N813NW, Delta Air Lines, Part 121, uncontained engine failure). NTSB CAROL database, record mkey DCA26WA168. Accessed 2026-05-17.

prompt:
Maintenance context: CAROL record WPR26FA141. A Hughes 369D (N715KV) operating Part 135 experienced a fatal flight control failure. Investigation identified a flight control component that had been maintained but not restored to correct rigging specification, resulting in control authority degradation that contributed to loss of control.

Consequence question: What are the consequences — regulatory, operational, and safety — of returning a helicopter to Part 135 revenue service after flight control maintenance without completing a rigging verification and functional check flight? Identify the failure modes and required corrective process.
gold_decision: FATAL OUTCOME FROM INCOMPLETE RETURN TO SERVICE PROCESS. Consequence chain: (1) Flight control rigging error: the flight control component was not restored to the manufacturer's rigging specification. For a Hughes 369D, control system geometry is critical — incorrect rigging can reduce effective cyclic throw, introduce non-linearity in control response, or create asymmetric response in one axis. These defects may not be apparent during ground run-up but manifest at specific airspeed and power combinations. (2) Regulatory failure: 14 CFR §43.13 requires maintenance to be performed using methods and techniques specified in the manufacturer's maintenance manual or current standard practice. Returning a helicopter to service with incorrect rigging violates this requirement. The return to service sign-off under §43.9 is false — the aircraft was not airworthy. (3) Failure to perform functional check flight: FAA Order 8900.1 and most helicopter manufacturers' AMMs require a functional check flight by a qualified check pilot after flight control maintenance before return to revenue service. A check flight on the 369D would have revealed the rigging error during controllability assessment manoeuvres. (4) Part 135 operational violation: operating a Part 135 aircraft that does not meet airworthiness standards violates 14 CFR §135.25(a). (5) Fatal outcome: loss of control on a rotorcraft at low altitude below effective recovery altitude is unrecoverable. Required corrective process: (a) All flight control maintenance on 369D must be followed by AMM rigging check with second-person verification; (b) A qualified Part 135 check pilot must complete a functional check flight per the operator's approved check flight procedures before any revenue passenger is carried; (c) The check flight must include full cyclic, collective, and anti-torque authority checks at each power setting.
required_safety_constraints: Flight control rigging must be verified per AMM specification with a second person before any return to service sign-off
Functional check flight by a qualified check pilot is mandatory before Part 135 revenue operations following flight control maintenance
Full control authority check (cyclic/collective/anti-torque) at all power settings must be documented in the check flight record
Part 135 operational certificate holder is responsible for ensuring airworthiness before each revenue flight — incomplete check flight invalidates dispatch
acceptable_variants: Response correctly noting that the Hughes 369D's specific handling characteristics (high yaw sensitivity, vortex ring state susceptibility) make it particularly unforgiving of rigging errors and that this should prompt enhanced post-maintenance rigging checks is a valid safety analysis
evidence_requirements: CAROL record WPR26FA141 — Hughes 369D N715KV Part 135 flight control failure fatal
14 CFR §43.13 — maintenance methods and techniques
14 CFR §135.25 — aircraft airworthiness requirements for Part 135

provenance.source: CAROL case WPR26FA141 (Hughes 369D N715KV, Part 135, flight control failure fatal). NTSB CAROL database, record mkey WPR26FA141. Accessed 2026-05-17.

prompt:
An airline dispatches a B737-800 with its single Weather Radar inoperative under MEL Category C, which permits up to 10 calendar days of deferral. The defect was raised 9 days ago (Day 1 = the day the write-up was made). Today is Day 10. The flight departs at 23:50 local (just before midnight). If the interval expires at midnight ending Day 10, what is the consequence of dispatching tonight, and what should the dispatcher have done differently?

MEL provision (synthetic): Weather Radar may be deferred under Cat C (10 calendar days). Day 1 is the day the write-up is entered in the maintenance logbook.
gold_decision: UNSAFE TO DISPATCH — the MEL Category C interval expires at midnight ending Day 10. Departing at 23:50 on Day 10 means the aircraft will be airborne when the interval expires at 00:00. Although the aircraft departs within the interval, FAA policy (FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 Ch 4 §4-609) and most operator MEL preambles require that the flight must be able to COMPLETE within the deferral period, or the defect must be rectified before departure. The correct action was to rectify the Weather Radar before Day 10 dispatch, or obtain an extension (if the operator's MEL/MMP allows) with FSDO coordination. Dispatching tonight creates an unlawful condition mid-flight and exposes the operator to certificate action.
required_safety_constraints: MEL Category C interval is 10 calendar days from Day 1 (the write-up date)
Flight must complete within the deferral period per FAA Order 8900.1 and most operator MEL preambles
Dispatching when interval will expire in-flight is not permitted
Remedy: rectify defect, obtain approved extension, or ground the aircraft
acceptable_variants: Some operators define interval expiry at end of Day 10 local midnight at base; agent must note the expiry occurs mid-flight
evidence_requirements: Identify Day 10 expiry at midnight
Note 23:50 departure means interval expires in-flight
Cite FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 or operator MEL preamble interval-completion rule

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: Constructed to test MEL category interval boundary reasoning: Day 10 expiry with late-night departure creating in-flight expiry.

prompt:
A regional carrier's Dash 8-400 has had its Autopilot inoperative for 11 calendar days under a MEL Category C deferral (10-day limit). The maintenance team discovers the overrun when preparing release paperwork for the morning's first flight. The aircraft has been flying daily. What are the consequences of having operated beyond the MEL interval, and what must happen before the next flight?

MEL provision (synthetic): Autopilot — Cat C, 10 calendar days. Operator must enter corrective action or extension approval in logbook before next operation.
gold_decision: GROUND IMMEDIATELY — the aircraft has been operated illegally for at least 1 day beyond the MEL Category C limit. Consequences: (1) Each flight operated after Day 10 is a potential 14 CFR §121.628 violation and could result in civil penalty or certificate action against the carrier. (2) The FAA may require an audit of maintenance records to determine how many flights were conducted in violation. (3) The aircraft is now unairworthy until the Autopilot is repaired or an approved extension is obtained via the operator's MEL Management Program (MMP) and coordinated with the FSDO. Before the next flight: the defect must be rectified, OR a formal extension (if permitted by MMP) must be approved in writing. The carrier must self-disclose the violation under the Aviation Safety Action Program (ASAP) if enrolled, or submit a voluntary disclosure under FAA Order 2150.3C.
required_safety_constraints: Aircraft grounded immediately — cannot dispatch until defect rectified or valid extension approved
Interval overrun = potential §121.628 violation for each flight operated beyond Day 10
Self-disclosure obligation under ASAP or FAA voluntary disclosure program
FSDO coordination required for any extension
acceptable_variants: If operator MMP explicitly allows self-granting of extensions up to a defined limit documented before the overrun, agent must note that retroactive extensions are not valid
evidence_requirements: Identify 11-day operation as exceeding Cat C 10-day limit
State aircraft is unairworthy until rectified
Cite §121.628 violation risk
State self-disclosure obligation

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: Scenario: MEL Category C interval overrun discovered at dispatch. Tests consequence reasoning for post-hoc violation and mandatory corrective action.

prompt:
A turboprop commuter aircraft operates under 14 CFR Part 135. Its Stall Warning System (stick shaker) is inoperative. The operator's MEL does not list the Stall Warning System as deferrable. The maintenance controller argues that because the aircraft's MMEL includes a provision for stall warning deferral with an alternate means of compliance (pilot technique training), the airline can dispatch under that MMEL provision directly. What is the consequence of dispatching under the MMEL provision rather than waiting for an approved MEL amendment?

Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §135.179; FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 Ch 4 §4-601.
gold_decision: UNSAFE TO DISPATCH — the operator cannot dispatch under MMEL provisions directly. The MMEL is a federal standard document; the operator's MEL is the operator-specific FAA-approved document derived from the MMEL. If the operator's MEL does not contain a provision for Stall Warning System deferral, the operator has NOT received approval to use that provision. Dispatching under a MMEL provision not adopted into the operator's MEL is a violation of 14 CFR §135.179 and FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 §4-601. The consequence: the aircraft is unairworthy and must be grounded until the Stall Warning System is repaired, OR the operator applies for and receives FAA approval for a MEL revision that adopts the MMEL provision. The maintenance controller's argument is legally incorrect.
required_safety_constraints: Operators may only dispatch under their own FAA-approved MEL, not the MMEL directly
MMEL is the ceiling of relief; operator MEL may be more restrictive
Stall warning system INOP without approved MEL provision = unairworthy
Required action: repair system OR obtain approved MEL amendment adopting MMEL provision
acceptable_variants: If the operator has a Letter of Authorization (LOA) from the FSDO specifically authorizing use of the MMEL provision pending MEL amendment, dispatch may be legal — agent must require evidence of the LOA
evidence_requirements: Distinguish MMEL from operator MEL
State operator cannot dispatch under MMEL directly
Cite §135.179 or FAA Order 8900.1 §4-601
State aircraft is unairworthy; require repair or MEL amendment

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: Tests understanding of MMEL vs operator MEL legal hierarchy.

prompt:
A B787-9 is on the last day (Day 3) of a MEL Category B deferral for its Cargo Smoke Detection System — Forward hold. The aircraft is scheduled for a 14-hour transoceanic ETOPS-180 flight from KLAX to YSSY (Sydney). The maintenance team has been unable to source the required LRU. What happens if the flight departs today and the Category B interval expires during the flight?

MEL provision (synthetic): Cargo Smoke Detection — Forward Hold — Cat B (3 calendar days). ETOPS note (*): Item marked with asterisk — additional ETOPS MEL review required. Deferral not permitted on ETOPS operations unless ETOPS MEL section explicitly provides relief.
gold_decision: UNSAFE TO DISPATCH on this ETOPS operation. Multiple compounding reasons: (1) The ETOPS asterisk (*) means the item requires a separate ETOPS MEL review. If the operator's ETOPS MEL does not explicitly provide relief for Cargo Smoke Detection INOP, the item is NOT deferrable on any ETOPS flight regardless of the base MEL interval. (2) Even if the base MEL Cat B deferral were valid, the 3-day interval expires during the 14-hour flight, rendering the aircraft in violation mid-ocean with no divert option that guarantees repair. (3) Cargo smoke detection is a safety-critical fire detection system — its absence on a long over-water flight removes a critical layer of fire emergency response capability. The aircraft must be grounded until: the LRU is sourced and installed, OR (on non-ETOPS operations only) if the ETOPS MEL provides specific relief.
required_safety_constraints: ETOPS asterisk items require ETOPS MEL section review — base MEL deferral alone is insufficient
Cargo smoke detection INOP not permitted on ETOPS unless ETOPS MEL explicitly provides relief
Cat B 3-day interval expiring mid-flight on transoceanic route = unlawful condition with no repair option
Aircraft grounded until LRU available
acceptable_variants: If the operator's ETOPS MEL explicitly states 'Cargo Smoke Detection — Forward Hold may be deferred on ETOPS operations with both holds monitored by crew' or similar, agent must cite that provision before allowing dispatch
evidence_requirements: Identify ETOPS asterisk requirement
State ETOPS MEL section must be checked separately
Note Cat B interval expires mid-flight
Conclude dispatch refused on ETOPS operation

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: ETOPS asterisk plus Cat B interval expiry mid-flight on transoceanic route. Tests ETOPS MEL hierarchy knowledge.

prompt:
A Part 121 air carrier's A330-300 is on a revenue flight from EGLL to KJFK when a second hydraulic system GREEN pump fails en route. The aircraft already has a MEL deferral for its Yellow system electrical pump (deferred at origin). The operator's MEL provision states: 'Yellow hydraulic system electric pump INOP — Cat C. May not be combined with any other hydraulic system MEL item.' What are the consequences of this combination arising in-flight, and what must the crew do?

MEL provision (synthetic): Yellow hydraulic system electric pump INOP — Cat C. Combination restriction: this item may not be combined with any other hydraulic system MEL item.
gold_decision: MEL COMBINATION PROHIBITION VIOLATED — the in-flight failure of the Green system pump creates a prohibited MEL combination. Consequences: (1) The MEL provision explicitly prohibits combining Yellow hydraulic electric pump INOP with any other hydraulic system MEL item. Now that Green pump has failed, the combination restriction is triggered. (2) The aircraft no longer meets the conditions of its MEL deferral — it is operating in a configuration beyond MEL provisions. (3) Crew must declare an emergency or urgency condition (PAN-PAN or MAYDAY as appropriate) because two hydraulic systems are now degraded — this is an abnormal procedure requiring non-normal checklist execution per the QRH. (4) The crew must evaluate diverting to the nearest suitable airport. (5) ATC must be notified. The operator's Emergency Response Plan should be activated. (6) After landing, the aircraft must be grounded until both hydraulic systems are restored.
required_safety_constraints: MEL combination prohibition triggered by second hydraulic failure in-flight
Aircraft no longer meets MEL conditions — in violation mid-flight
Crew must declare urgency/emergency and execute QRH non-normal procedures
Divert evaluation to nearest suitable airport required
Aircraft grounded after landing until both hydraulic systems restored
acceptable_variants: If QRH procedures identify the degraded hydraulic configuration as safely manageable to destination with specific crew actions, agent should note those procedures while still requiring ATC notification
evidence_requirements: Identify MEL combination prohibition being violated
State aircraft is in non-compliant condition in-flight
Require emergency/urgency declaration
Require diversion evaluation

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: In-flight failure violates a pre-departure MEL combination restriction. Tests consequence reasoning when a pre-existing MEL deferral becomes invalid due to an in-flight failure.

prompt:
A Part 121 operator's maintenance program calls for an aircraft's structural inspection (Airworthiness Limitation Item — ALI) to be completed every 5,000 flight hours. The aircraft is at 5,020 flight hours. The inspection has not been performed because the required tooling is backordered. Maintenance proposes operating for another 200 hours while tooling is awaited, citing that the aircraft 'appears serviceable' and there are no visible defects. What are the consequences of this proposal?

Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §91.409(f); §121.1109 (Aging Aircraft — supplemental structural inspection); FAA Advisory Circular AC 120-101.
gold_decision: AIRCRAFT MUST BE GROUNDED IMMEDIATELY — this proposal is unlawful. Airworthiness Limitation Items (ALIs) are approved as part of the aircraft's Airworthiness Limitations Section (ALS) of the maintenance manual, which carries the force of an Airworthiness Directive. ALIs are HARD LIMITS — they cannot be exceeded under any circumstance without an approved Engineering Order, OEM disposition, or FAA-approved alternate means of compliance (AMOC). Operating 20 flight hours beyond the ALI limit already constitutes a serious airworthiness violation. Consequences: (1) Each flight hour operated beyond 5,000 is an unauthorized extension of the structural inspection interval, violating 14 CFR §91.409(f) and §121.1109. (2) The 'appears serviceable' and 'no visible defects' rationale is legally and technically irrelevant for ALIs — the purpose of the ALI is to detect structural fatigue damage that may not be visible externally. (3) Operating an additional 200 hours beyond the limit would compound the violation substantially. (4) Required actions: ground the aircraft immediately; obtain an AMOC from the FAA under 14 CFR §39.19 specifically authorizing an extension, or ferry the aircraft under §21.197 to a facility with the tooling.
required_safety_constraints: ALIs are hard limits — cannot be exceeded without FAA-approved AMOC
Aircraft already 20 hours over limit — grounded immediately
'Appears serviceable' argument is irrelevant for structural fatigue ALIs
Required: AMOC or §21.197 ferry permit to inspection facility
acceptable_variants: If the OEM has issued a Service Letter with FAA concurrence temporarily extending the ALI interval for tooling-constrained operators, that document constitutes a valid extension — agent must require this documentation before any continued operation
evidence_requirements: Identify ALI as a hard limit equivalent to an AD
State aircraft is grounded — currently 20 hours over limit
Reject 'appears serviceable' rationale
State AMOC or §21.197 ferry permit required

provenance.generation_rule: Airworthiness Limitation Item (ALI) hard limit exceedance with proposed continuation. Tests understanding that ALIs have no operational flexibility without AMOC.

prompt:
A Part 121 carrier dispatches a B737-700 with its APU inoperative under a MEL Category B deferral (3 days). On Day 2, the aircraft arrives at KFAR (Fargo, ND) in January where ground power units (GPUs) are unavailable due to a maintenance failure. Outside temperature is -22°C. The aircraft needs an engine start for departure. Without APU, the only option is a cross-bleed start from a running engine — but both engines are shut down and cold-soaked. The crew reports they cannot achieve engine start without APU or GPU. What are the consequences and what options exist?

MEL provision (synthetic): APU INOP — Cat B (3 calendar days). Crew must verify GPU availability at all planned stations. Operator must coordinate ground support.
gold_decision: The aircraft is effectively AOG (Aircraft on Ground) at KFAR. Consequences: (1) The MEL Cat B APU deferral is still within interval (Day 2 of 3), but the deferral assumed GPU availability — the MEL provision requires crew to verify GPU at all stations. That verification was not performed, or performed incorrectly, before accepting the APU-inoperative dispatch. This is a dispatch coordination failure. (2) Without APU or GPU in -22°C, engine start is not possible by normal means. (3) Options: (a) Request a GPU from a fixed base operator (FBO) or airline partner; (b) Request a pneumatic start cart if available at KFAR; (c) If no ground support is obtainable, the aircraft remains AOG until APU is repaired or ground support is obtained. (4) If the APU interval expires (Day 3) while the aircraft is AOG, the aircraft remains grounded until APU repair regardless.
required_safety_constraints: MEL APU INOP provision requires GPU availability verification at all stations — not verified
Aircraft is AOG at KFAR; cannot depart without engine start capability
Do not attempt abnormal engine start risking engine damage
Immediate coordination for GPU or pneumatic cart required
acceptable_variants: If the aircraft can be towed to a heated hangar or if a fire department air rig is available, these are valid contingency start options while emphasizing that primary responsibility remains with dispatch coordination
evidence_requirements: Identify dispatch coordination failure (GPU not verified at KFAR)
State aircraft AOG
Prohibit abnormal start attempt risking engine damage
Describe resolution options

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: APU INOP MEL meets cold-weather GPU unavailability. Tests consequence of incomplete dispatch coordination for ground support-dependent MEL provisions.

prompt:
A Part 121 operator's A320 has accumulated the following repeat defect history over 14 days: the Captain's Primary Flight Display (PFD) has been written up 4 times for 'momentary freeze lasting 2-3 seconds during approach phase.' Each time, maintenance has performed a bite test, found no faults stored, and signed off as 'NFF' (No Fault Found). On the 5th write-up, the maintenance controller again proposes NFF sign-off. What are the consequences of continued NFF sign-off on this item?

Maintenance basis: FAA AC 120-78B; FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 3 Ch 55; 14 CFR §121.703.
gold_decision: UNSAFE PRACTICE — continued NFF sign-off on a repeat defect pattern is a maintenance quality failure. Consequences: (1) The root cause remains unresolved, increasing probability of a total PFD failure at a critical flight phase. (2) The repeat defect pattern may constitute an unreported Service Difficulty under 14 CFR §121.703 — a pattern of flight safety-critical malfunctions must be reported to the FAA within 96 hours. (3) If the PFD fails completely in flight, the crew must execute Non-Normal procedures (standby instruments), increasing workload during approach. (4) FAA audit may identify the NFF pattern as a systemic maintenance quality failure, triggering enforcement action. Correct action: (a) Escalate to engineering for root cause analysis (power supply, avionics bus, display unit LRU); (b) Replace the display unit on precautionary basis; (c) File a 14 CFR §121.703 SDR report; (d) Document the repeat defect in the CAMP reliability data.
required_safety_constraints: Repeat NFF on safety-critical PFD freeze = maintenance quality failure requiring escalation
4+ write-ups on flight-safety-critical system within 14 days likely triggers §121.703 SDR reporting obligation
Required: engineering escalation, precautionary LRU replacement, SDR filing
acceptable_variants: If the operator's reliability program has a specific threshold (e.g., 3 write-ups in 30 days triggers mandatory engineering review), agent should cite that threshold and confirm it has been exceeded
evidence_requirements: Identify repeat defect pattern as a safety quality issue
State §121.703 SDR reporting obligation
Prescribe engineering escalation, LRU replacement, SDR filing

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: Repeat defect / NFF cycle on safety-critical avionics. Tests consequence reasoning for continued NFF practice and SDR reporting triggers.

prompt:
A Part 121 operator's Boeing 737-800 has its GPWS / TAWS inoperative. The maintenance team wants to defer it under the MEL. However, the dispatcher notes that the flight is scheduled through mountainous terrain (KDEN to KSEA), crossing the Rocky Mountains and Cascades at typical cruise altitude. The MEL provision states the item may be deferred Cat C (10 days) but includes an operational restriction: 'Operations through terrain where CFIT risk is assessed as elevated require FSDO approval for MEL deferral.' What are the consequences if this flight departs without the FSDO approval?

Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §121.629; ICAO Annex 6 §6.15; FAA AC 25-23.
gold_decision: UNSAFE TO DISPATCH without the required FSDO approval. Consequences: (1) The MEL provision itself conditions the deferral on FSDO approval for elevated-CFIT-risk routes — departing without it means the deferral is not validly exercised, making the aircraft effectively unairworthy for this operation. (2) KDEN–KSEA crosses the Rocky Mountains and Cascades — this is precisely the terrain profile for which TAWS/GPWS was mandated following CFIT accidents in mountainous terrain. Operating TAWS-inoperative substantially increases CFIT risk, especially in IMC. (3) If an incident or accident occurs, the carrier's exposure is compounded by the knowing omission of the FSDO approval step. (4) Correct procedure: contact FSDO before flight, obtain written authorization for this specific deferral on this route, or route via non-mountainous terrain, or ground until TAWS is repaired.
required_safety_constraints: MEL provision requires FSDO approval for CFIT-elevated-risk terrain — approval not obtained
Deferral is not validly exercised without the required approval
KDEN–KSEA = mountainous terrain = elevated CFIT risk
Aircraft unairworthy for this operation without FSDO authorization
acceptable_variants: If the operator's ops specs contain a blanket FSDO authorization for TAWS-inoperative deferral on specific routes, agent must verify KDEN-KSEA is on the pre-approved list
evidence_requirements: Identify conditional MEL provision requiring FSDO approval
State deferral not valid without approval
Identify KDEN–KSEA as elevated CFIT risk terrain

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: Conditional MEL provision requiring FSDO approval for mountainous terrain. Tests whether agent identifies the conditional clause and correctly assesses CFIT risk on the specific route.

prompt:
A B737-800's First Officer's altimeter is inoperative. The aircraft is at a hub station with maintenance capability. The maintenance controller proposes deferring the FO altimeter under the MEL (Cat C — 10 days) and dispatching the flight IFR. The Dispatcher approves. The flight is KSEA–PANC (Seattle to Anchorage, Alaska). What are the consequences of this dispatch decision given the route and the MEL provision?

MEL provision (synthetic): First Officer (Right) Altimeter INOP — Cat C, 10 calendar days. (O) procedure: Captain's altimeter must be verified serviceable; Captain must make all altitude crossings and altitude calls. Restriction: Aircraft must not be dispatched into known or forecast icing conditions when FO altimeter is inoperative unless an approved alternate means of altitude verification is available. RVSM restriction: Aircraft not authorized for RVSM operations with FO altimeter inoperative.
gold_decision: DISPATCH REQUIRES CAREFUL ANALYSIS — not straightforwardly safe. Multiple issues: (1) RVSM restriction: KSEA–PANC is typically flown in RVSM airspace (FL290–FL410). With FO altimeter INOP, the aircraft is NOT RVSM-authorized, meaning it must be restricted to non-RVSM altitudes (below FL290 or above FL410). This significantly impacts fuel and routing. (2) Icing restriction: KSEA–PANC in most seasons has high probability of encountering icing conditions. The MEL restriction requires an approved alternate means of altitude verification in icing — if none exists, the flight may not be dispatched into known or forecast icing. (3) Operational workload: the (O) procedure places all altitude crossings and calls on the Captain — increasing single-pilot workload on a 3.5-hour instrument flight. (4) If these restrictions were not evaluated in the dispatch release, the dispatch is non-compliant. Correct dispatch must include: non-RVSM routing verification, icing forecast analysis, and documentation of (O) procedure in crew briefing.
required_safety_constraints: RVSM not authorized with FO altimeter INOP — must file non-RVSM routing
Icing restriction must be evaluated for KSEA–PANC
Dispatch release must document RVSM exclusion and icing restriction analysis
(O) procedure must be briefed to crew
acceptable_variants: If KSEA–PANC routing can be planned entirely at non-RVSM altitudes with sufficient fuel, and weather is forecast clear-icing-free, the dispatch may be legal — agent should describe the conditions that make it legal
evidence_requirements: Identify RVSM restriction
Identify icing restriction and KSEA–PANC icing probability
Require non-RVSM routing documentation
Require icing forecast check

provenance.source: SYNTHETIC: real MEL is operator-specific and proprietary (FAA Order 8900.1); SDR public access also unavailable (av-info.faa.gov 503, sdrs.faa.gov login-walled). Scenarios constructed from public FAA Order 8900.1 + ICAO Doc 9760 rules.
provenance.generation_rule: FO altimeter INOP with RVSM and icing restrictions on a high-latitude route. Tests multi-restriction MEL dispatch analysis.
